coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric determination of amifostine and alkaline phosphatase on amphiprotic molecularly imprinted silica crosslinked with binary functional silanes and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ke, Ching-Bin
Lu, Te-Ling
Chen, Jian-Lian
coredata.dc:description: A silica-based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) formed by functional silanes (basic 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and acidic 2-(4-chlorosulfonylphenyl)ethyltrimethoxysilane (CSPTMS)) was crosslinked with carbon dots (CDs) to develop a fluorescent sensor toward an amphiprotic template, amifostine (AMF). The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of succinic acid and an ionic liquid and possessed hydroxyl and pyrrolic functional groups, which enabled the CDs to be derivatized with silanes for subsequent sol-gel polymerization. Except for the CDs derivatized with tetraethoxysilane, CD-APTES, CD-CSPTMS, and CD-APTES/CSPTMS (molar ratio = 1/1) all presented distinct fluorescence dynamic quenching when interacted with AMF. However, APTES/CSPTMS was selected as the sol-gel monomer for the formation of MIP because its quenching ratio and imprinted factor were the highest among the CD-silane-MIPs. Moreover, 0.5 mg/mL of CD-APTES/CSPTMS -MIP in pH 7.5 buffer was used to quantify AMF (0.5–200 nM, LOD = 0.15 nM) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) (2–150 μU/mL, LOD = 0.5 μU/mL), which activates the metabolism of AMF, and the calibration curves of AMF and ALP were determined via fluorescence quenching and restoration, respectively. The recoveries of 1, 10, and 60 nM AMF from 360-fold-diluted human serum solutions were 95, 104, and 103%, respectively, with RSD values that were lower than 4.2%. The average ALP activity of the original human serum was determined to be 32.1 U/L (RSD = 5.41%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amifostine
Amphiprotic
APTES
Carbon dot
CSPTMS
Human serum
Ionic liquid
Imprinted
Phosphatase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence measurement of AMF and ALP using CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP
Analysis of AMF and ALP in human serum samples
Characterization of the CDs, CD-silanes, CD-MIP, and CD-NIP
Fluorescent sensing of AMF and ALP using CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the preparation of CDs and the silanized CDs, including (A) CD-APTES, (B) CD-CSPTMS, (C) CD-APTES/CSPTMS, and (D) CD-TEOS.

Characterization of the CDs. (A) HR-TEM image, scale bar = 20 nm, (B) HR-TEM image, scale bar = 5 nm (3 × 107 X), (C) DLS data, (D) XRD spectrum, (E) decay and biexponential fitted curves from the time-resolved fluorescence (λem = 450 nm and λex = 380 nm), and (F) XPS spectrum.

Fluorescence spectra of the (A) CDs, (B) CD-silanes, and (C) CD-MIPs. The red-dotted curve in (A) is the UV-Vis absorption spectrum. The black curves in (B) and (C) are the spectra of CD-silanes, (a) CD-APTES, (b) CD-CSPTMS, (c) CD-APTES/CSPTMS, and (d) CD-TEOS. The blue curves in (B) are the spectra of the CDs, and those in (C) are the MIP-CDs corresponding to the respective CD-silanes. The red curves in (B) and (C) are the respective spectra of CD-silanes and MIP-CDs after adding 60 nM AMF. The quenching ratios of CD-MIPs (■) and CD-NIPs (□) and their imprinted factors were measured at λem = 475 nm and λex = 380 nm and are plotted in (D).
Synthesis scheme and SEM and TEM (insert) images of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP.
Effect of pH (A, ○), probe concentration (B, Δ), and response time (C, □) on the quenching ratio of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP after adding 60 nM AMF. (λem = 475 nm and λex = 380 nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP with the addition of AMF at different concentrations. (B) Relationship between F 0/F and the concentration of AMF. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP (1.5 mg) added to solutions of 0.1 mL of 9 μM AMF, 0.1 mL of ALP at different concentrations, and 2.8 mL of 50 mM phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.5). F 0 and F were measured at 475 nm with 380 nm excitation. (D) Relationship between F and the concentration of ALP.

head.ce:title: Fluorometric determination of amifostine and alkaline phosphatase on amphiprotic molecularly imprinted silica crosslinked with binary functional silanes and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Beauty and Health Care, Min-Hwei Junior College of Health Care Management, No.1116, Sec 2, Zhongshan E. Rd., Tainan, 73658, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Beauty and Health Care
Min-Hwei Junior College of Health Care Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Beauty and Health Care, Min-Hwei Junior College of Health Care Management, No.1116, Sec 2, Zhongshan E. Rd., Tainan 73658, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, China Medical University, No. 91 Hsueh-Shih Road, Taichung, 40402, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, China Medical University, No. 91 Hsueh-Shih Road, Taichung 40402, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A silica-based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) formed by functional silanes (basic 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and acidic 2-(4-chlorosulfonylphenyl)ethyltrimethoxysilane (CSPTMS)) was crosslinked with carbon dots (CDs) to develop a fluorescent sensor toward an amphiprotic template, amifostine (AMF). The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of succinic acid and an ionic liquid and possessed hydroxyl and pyrrolic functional groups, which enabled the CDs to be derivatized with silanes for subsequent sol-gel polymerization. Except for the CDs derivatized with tetraethoxysilane, CD-APTES, CD-CSPTMS, and CD-APTES/CSPTMS (molar ratio = 1/1) all presented distinct fluorescence dynamic quenching when interacted with AMF. However, APTES/CSPTMS was selected as the sol-gel monomer for the formation of MIP because its quenching ratio and imprinted factor were the highest among the CD-silane-MIPs. Moreover, 0.5 mg/mL of CD-APTES/CSPTMS -MIP in pH 7.5 buffer was used to quantify AMF (0.5–200 nM, LOD = 0.15 nM) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) (2–150 μU/mL, LOD = 0.5 μU/mL), which activates the metabolism of AMF, and the calibration curves of AMF and ALP were determined via fluorescence quenching and restoration, respectively. The recoveries of 1, 10, and 60 nM AMF from 360-fold-diluted human serum solutions were 95, 104, and 103%, respectively, with RSD values that were lower than 4.2%. The average ALP activity of the original human serum was determined to be 32.1 U/L (RSD = 5.41%).

ce:section.ce:para: The combination of the recognition ability of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with the physical properties of nanoparticles has shown great potential for solid-phase extraction (SPE), catalysis, and (bio)chemical sensing (Ansari, 2017; Dabrowski et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018; Keçili and Hussain, 2018). Carbon-based nanodots (CDs), which possess excellent luminescent properties, surface functionality, biocompatibility, aqueous dispersibility, and preparation simplicity and variety (Yan et al., 2018; Molaei, 2019), have also been conjugated with various MIPs. This conjugation is achieved by sol-gel processes, polymerization of vinyl compounds (Feng et al., 2015; Patra et al., 2017), dopamine (Jalili and Amjadi, 2018), and indolyl boric acid (Zhou et al., 2017), and electrodeposition on an electrode by cyclic potential sweep (Pandey and Jha, 2015; Pandey and Kant, 2016; Yang et al., 2017a; Jin et al., 2018). CD-MIP composites have been successfully applied in SPE (Liang et al., 2018) and in electrochemical (Ensafi et al., 2018a; Pandey and Jha, 2015; Pandey and Kant, 2016), electrochemiluminescent (Yang et al., 2017a; Jin et al., 2018), and fluorescent sensing.

For fluorescent sensing, the sol-gel method is the most popular conjugation method owing to its easy processing and stable formation of MIPs (Lofgreen and Ozin, 2014). In the beginning, some functional organosilanes were mixed with carbonaceous materials for the production of the CDs functionalized with methoxysilyl groups on CD surfaces by hydrothermal carbonation (Mao et al., 2012; Jalili and Amjadi, 2015; Amjadi and Jalili, 2017; Bhogal et al., 2019). The methoxysilyl groups enable the CDs to be further copolymerized with sol-gel monomers, including the functional silane and the constitutional and crosslinking silane, tetraethoxysilane. The most recently developed approach was derivatization of bare CDs with functional silanes before sol-gel polymerization (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018a). Thereafter, the functionalized CDs can be condensed with sol-gel monomers. For MIP sol-gel processes, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) has been the most widely used functional silane, whereas a few processes have involved N-β-(aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane instead (Mao et al., 2012; Bhogal et al., 2019). The structures of the two functional silanes are very similar, and both bear a primary amine group for which the positively charged hydrogen atoms of the hydrated ammonium moiety provide imprinted templates with ion-induced dipole interactions, hydrogen bond donors, and cationic binding sites. However, there has been no study on negatively charged functional groups incorporated in sol-gel MIP frameworks until now. Not using negatively charged groups is disadvantageous for templates that require the hydrogen bond acceptors and anionic bonding sites to be properly fitted into the MIP cavities.

Amifostine (AMF), an amphiprotic amino thiophosphate, is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration for use as a cytoprotective agent against the renal toxicity of cisplatin in patients with cancer that are undergoing chemotherapy and radiotherapy. The protection activity of AMF originates from the thiol group of its dephosphorylated metabolite, amifostine thiol, which is formed by the dephosphorylation of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) found in normal tissue but not in tumor tissue (Bachy et al., 2004; Facorro et al., 2004). Therefore, it is necessary to measure the AMF levels to improve tumor control and quality of life of cancer patients and to measure the ALP activity to indicate the physiological and pathological states. For a long time, most measurements were based on analysis of the WR-1065 peak in liquid chromatography (LC) coupled with electrochemical detection or fluorescent derivatization (Bai et al., 2002; Chen et al., 2005). Recently, fluorescent nanosensors have been designed to measure the data in a simpler, more rapid, and more sensitive manner than LC (Na et al., 2015; Li et al., 2018b; Na et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019). However, only CdS quantum dots have been used to directly detect AMF in the absence of ALP according to the resulting increase in fluorescence intensity (FL), whereas later addition of ALP causes a decrease in FL accordingly (Na et al., 2015). Other nanosensors have been based on the detection of WR-1065; thus, either AMF or ALP can be indirectly detected (Li et al., 2018b; Na et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019).

In this study, an amphiprotic silica-based CD-MIP crosslinked with binary functional silanes, APTES and 2-(4-chlorosulfonylphenyl)ethyltrimethoxysilane (CSPTMS), was prepared and compared with CD-MIPs crosslinked with unitary functional silanes. The synthesis and characterization of CDs, CD-silanes, and their corresponding CD-MIPs are presented to validate their sensitivity and selectivity toward AMF. CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP, which exhibited the best performance and was used to optimize the sensing conditions, was successfully used in the analysis of AMF and ALP in human serum.

Succinic acid, acetone, diethyl ether, ethanol, ammonia water, hydrogen chloride, amifostine trihydrate (WR2721; S-2-(3-aminopropylamino)ethylphosphorothioic acid; AMF), trichloroacetic acid, and 2-(4-chlorosulfonylphenyl)ethyltrimethoxysilane (CSPTMS) were purchased from Acros Organics (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Geel, Belgium). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide ([EMIM]N(CN)2), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), sodium orthovanadate, and human serum were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). All phosphate salts were of analytical grade and were obtained from Aldrich and Acros. Purified water (18 MΩ cm) obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used to prepare the standard salt and buffer solutions. All standard solutions were protected from light and maintained at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The diluted aqueous solutions were characterized by a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Panalytical, UK), ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrometer (Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, Cambridge, MA, USA), spectrofluorometer (LS55, PerkinElmer), and time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system (LED source PLS-370 (380 ± 10 nm), PMT detector, TimeHarp 260, PicoQuant, Berlin, Germany).

Some product solutions were dried at 70 °C in a vacuum oven for 24 h to prepare solid samples for characterization via field-emission scanning electron microscopy (JSM-7800F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) using a system (JEM-2100, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, high-resolution X-ray diffractometer (copper anode, D8 Discover, Bruker, MA, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy system (ULVAC-PHI PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Physical Electronics, Eden Prairie, MN, USA), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Prestige-21, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a single reflection horizontal ATR (attenuated total reflection) accessory (MIRacle, PIKE Technologies, Fitchburg, WI, USA).

First, 500 mg of succinic acid was stirred magnetically with 1.0 mL of [EMIM]N(CN)2 for 5.0 min. The ionic liquid solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined vessel, sealed in a stainless-steel autoclave, and placed in an oven at 250 °C for 1.0 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature naturally, 10 mL of water was added to the vessel to wash out the dark brown, sticky product. The product solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter composed of mixed cellulose esters, and the filtrate was washed with acetone and diethyl ether to remove impurities. The washed filtrate was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to precipitate large aggregates. Then, the supernatant was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a molecular weight cutoff membrane (3.5–5 kD) for 2 days. After vacuum freeze-drying the dialysate, 355 mg of solid CDs was obtained.

First, 50 mg of CDs and 2.0 mmol of silane were mixed and heated in a 25-mL round-bottom flask at 50 °C for 3 h. Four different silane solutions, namely, APTES, CSPTMS, APTES/CSPTMS (mol/mol = 1/1), and TEOS, were tested separately. After the solutions were cooled to ambient temperature, the CD-silane products, including CD-APTES, CD-CSPTMS, CD-APTES/CSPTMS, and CD-TEOS, were formed in the first, second, third, and fourth flasks, respectively. The reactions are depicted in Fig. 1 .

For sol-gel polymerization, 1 mmol of the AMF template and 1 mL of ethanol were added sequentially in each of the four flasks with magnetic stirring. This step was followed by slowly dropping 4.0 mmol of APTES and 4.0 mmol of TEOS in the first flask, 4.0 mmol of CSPTMS and 4.0 mmol of TEOS in the second flask, and 2.0 mmol of APTES, 2.0 mmol of CSPTMS, and 4.0 mmol of TEOS in the third and fourth flasks. Then, 0.2 mL of ammonia water (20%) was added to each flask to initiate polymerization. After stirring for 3 h at room temperature, the mixtures were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the precipitates were collected on 1.6-μm filter paper. The precipitates were continuously washed with ethanol and water until no AMF was indicated in the fluorescence test of the filtrates, in which a 20-μL aliquot of the filtrate was mixed with 3 mL of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 50 mM) for 5.0 min for each test. After drying in an oven at 50 °C for 1 h, the resulting solids were MIPs coated on CDs (CD-MIPs). For comparison with the CD-MIPs, nonimprinted polymers (NIPs) coated on the CDs (CD-NIPs) were synthesized under the same conditions without addition of the AMF template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement of AMF and ALP using CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Certain amounts of the CD-silanes, CD-MIPs, and CD-NIPs were dissolved in 3 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 50 mM) to reach the fluorescence intensity near the highest level, 1000 a.u. After fluorescence spectra were recorded, 60 nM AMF was added to the buffer solutions to obtain the quenching ratios, ΔF/F 0 × 100% = (F 0–F)/F 0 × 100%, and the imprinted factors, ΔF MIP/ΔF NIP, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of AMF, respectively. For optimization of the sensing conditions, different concentrations of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP (0.2–0.8 mg/mL) were dissolved in 3 mL of phosphate buffer at different pH values (pH 3.0–10, 50 mM). The fluorescence spectra of the prepared CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP solutions were recorded before the addition of 60 nM AMF and were monitored as a function of time after the addition. For AMF quantification, the fluorescence spectra of AMF at different concentrations (0.5–200 nM) were measured under the optimized conditions, which included 0.5 mg/mL CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP, pH 7.5, and at least 2 min to reach equilibrium.

For quantification of ALP (2–150 μU/mL, 0.1 mL), 9 μM AMF (0.1 mL) and 1.5 mg of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP were successively added to 2.8 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.5, 50 mM). After 10 min of equilibration, the fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AMF and ALP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1 mL of human serum was mixed with 3 mL of trichloroacetic acid (15%, w/v) to precipitate interfering proteins. The turbid mixture was shaken vigorously for 5 min and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. A 0.5 mL aliquot of the supernatant was diluted to 15 and 150 mL with phosphate buffer (pH 7.5, 50 mM) to produce deproteinized serum solutions with 1:120 and 1:1200 dilution factors. The 120-fold-diluted deproteinized serum (1.0 mL), sodium orthovanadate (0.1 μM), and AMF (1, 10, and 60 nM) were sequentially added to 3.0 mL of the buffer solution to evaluate the recovery of spiked AMF.

For evaluation of the ALP activity, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 μL of the 1200-fold-diluted deproteinized serum and 70, 60, 50, 40, and 30 μL of the standard ALP (60 μU/mL) were mixed to volumes of 0.1 mL, respectively. The 0.1-mL ALP samples were quantified according to the quantification of ALP described in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs, CD-silanes, CD-MIP, and CD-NIP

ce:section.ce:para: The particle sizes of the purified CDs synthesized hydrothermally from the mixture of succinic acid and [EMIM]N(CN)2 were observed using HR-TEM and DLS, as shown in Fig. 2 A–C. The TEM image presented in Fig. 2A was further analyzed using ImageJ freeware, resulting in an average particle size of 3.66 nm (σ = 1.31 nm), as shown in Fig. S1. Through DLS measurements, the intensity-weighted mean hydrodynamic diameter (Z-average) of the CDs, which were dispersed in phosphate buffer (pH 7.5, 50 mM), was determined to be 5.15 nm with a moderate polydiversity index (PDI = 0.132), as shown in Fig. 2C. Some of the TEM images show the lattice fringes, indicating a spacing of 0.34 nm between the graphene layers, as depicted in Fig. 2B and Fig. S1(e). The XRD pattern presented in Fig. 2D shows a distinct peak centered at 2θ = 25.8° (d = 0.344 nm) for the (002) facet of graphite and a broad diffraction peak at approximately 2θ = 20° representing the amorphous nature. Both the TEM and XRD results suggested that the synthesized CDs could be classified as carbon quantum dots rather than carbon nanodots (Cayuela et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 2E, the lifetime in the time-resolved fluorescence of the CDs was calculated to be τav = 5.05 ns upon 380 nm excitation and 450 nm emission by tail-fitting the TCSPC decay curve with a biexponential function, F(t) = A 1 exp (-t/τ1) + A 2 exp (-t/τ2), where A 1 and A 2 are the amplitude percentages (A 1 = 37.39%, A 2 = 62.61%) of the e(−t/τ1) and e(−t/τ2) components (τ1 = 9.033 ns, τ2 = 2.668 ns), respectively. The lifetime of 5.05 ns fell within range of 3.9 ns–6.56 ns found for other carbon quantum dots (Li et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 3 A, the UV-Vis absorption and static fluorescence spectra of the CDs present an n–π* transition between the lone pair electrons on the surface groups and the vacant orbitals of the CC backbone at approximately 360 nm and the highest photoluminescence (PL) intensity at 450 nm (λmax ) upon 380 nm excitation. The PL quantum yield was calculated to be 35%, as shown in Fig. S2. There should be many surface defects or surface states, which represent the hybridization of the carbon backbone and connected functional groups, on the CD surface because of the distinct excitation-dependent emissions in the excitation range between 320 nm and 440 nm (Zhu et al., 2015). The surface groups were revealed by XPS and FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 2F, the full scan of the XPS spectrum presents a larger quantity of nitrogen atoms sourced from [EMIM]N(CN)2 than oxygen atoms sourced from succinic acid. As shown in Fig. S3, the deconvoluted XPS spectra further confirmed that parts of the imidazole rings in [EMIM]N(CN)2 were pyrolyzed during hydrothermal carbonation and that functional groups, such as hydroxyl and pyrrolic groups, formed on the CD surface were capable of facilitating nucleophilic substitution with alkoxysilanes. A detailed discussion of the XPS spectra is presented below Fig. S3. The pyrolysis of imidazole and the formed functional groups were confirmed by FTIR spectra, as presented in Fig. S4.

As shown in Fig. 1, four different alkoxysilane solutions, namely, APTES, CSPTMS, APTES/CSPTMS (mol/mol = 1/1), and TEOS, were used to silanize the CDs before the subsequent sol-gel polymerization. Owing to the changes in the surface states and/or increases in particle sizes, compared to the bare CDs, the four silanized CDs showed a slight redshift (20–25 nm) in the emission spectra in phosphate buffer at pH 7.0, as shown in Fig. 3B. By checking the changes in the fluorescence intensity after adding 60 nM AMF to the silanized CDs, CD-TEOS exhibited a lower quenching ratio than that of the other three silanized CDs, as shown in Fig. 3B (d). The CDs in CD-TEOS were densely surrounded by the TEOS-formed siloxane backbone, which hindered the approach of AMF because TEOS acted as a crosslinker, whereas the other functional silanes were loosely bound to the CDs. The approach caused CD emission (τav = 5.048 ns, τ1 = 9.033 (37.39%), τ2 = 2.668 (62.61%)) dynamic quenching: the fluorescence lifetime of CD-APTES/CSPTMS decreased to τav = 4.753 ns (τ1 = 8.735 (36.99%), τ2 = 2.415 (63.01%)) at room temperature and decreased further to τav = 4.325 ns (τ1 = 7.908 (35.91%), τ2 = 2.318 (64.09%)) at a higher temperature of 35 °C, as shown in Fig. S5. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer did not cause quenching because there was no UV-Vis absorption for AMF; and therefore, no spectra overlapped with the emission spectra of the CDs and silanized CDs.

The silanized CD samples, CD-APTES and CD-CSPTMS, were further copolymerized with the sol-gel monomers of APTES/TEOS and CSPTMS/TEOS, respectively, whereas CD-APTES/CSPTMS and CD-TEOS were copolymerized with APTES/CSPTMS/TEOS. The AMF template was added to the sol-gel monomers, was involved in the copolymerization, and later was stripped from the formed sol-gel polymer. The obtained copolymers, CD-MIPs, showed few changes in their fluorescence properties compared to those of their respective silanized CDs, as shown in Fig. 3C. Here, copolymerization did not substantially alter the emission states of the original silanized CDs. The CD-MIPs were further evaluated for the quenching ratio and imprinted factor in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 50 mM) by spiking with 60 nM AMF. Compared to CD-silanes, the MIP coatings of the CD-MIPs reduced the accessibility of AMF to CDs and the consequent quenching. Fig. 3D shows that CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP was superior to the other three CD-MIPs, according to this evaluation. The imprinted cavities of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP were more attractive to the AMF template than the other CD-MIPs because both positively and negatively charged ends of the functional silanes, APTES and CSPTMS, were well positioned at regions adjacent to the inversely charged ends of the template functional groups, as schematically depicted in Fig. 4 . Using only APTES or CSPTMS as the functional silanes did not create amphiprotic cavities that were adequately shaped for the amphiprotic template, resulting in lower quenching ratios. Although both APTES and CSPTMS were used to shape the cavities in CD-TEOS-MIP, the quenching ratio of CD-TEOS-MIP was the lowest because its TEOS-silanized CD precursor, i.e., CD-TEOS, hindered the approach of AMF before sol-gel polymerization, as discussed in Section 3.1.2. The hindrance caused by the involvement of TEOS in the sol-gel polymerization also lowered the quenching ratios (<80) of the other three CD-MIPs compared to those of their respective CD-silanes, as shown in Fig. 3B.

For the CD-NIPs, their quenching ratios were comparable and similar to that for CD-TEOS. The CDs embedded in the CD-NIPs were almost completely wrapped in the siloxane framework, similar to the CDs embedded in CD-TEOS. There was little space for AMF to approach the CDs to quench fluorescence because no imprinted cavity was formed to guide the approach.

With its high imprinted factor, CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP was chosen to further study its operational conditions for application to the analysis of AMF in human serum. Fig. 4 also shows the SEM and TEM images of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP, in which the CDs were observed to be round-shaped granules and were clouded by MIPs. Thus, the following discussion is confined to CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of AMF and ALP using CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP

ce:section.ce:para: First, 50 mM phosphate buffer solutions, whose pH values ranged from 3 to 10, were prepared to investigate the effect of pH on the interaction between AMF and CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP. Then, 0.4 mg/mL CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP samples were conditioned in the prepared buffers at room temperature, and their fluorescence intensities were measured at a 475-nm emission with 380-nm excitation after 60 nM AMF was added to the buffer solutions and equilibrated for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the quenching ratios reached a maximum at pH 7.5 and decreased at pH values above and lower than 7.5. At pH 7.5, the ionic dissociation of amino and sulfonic acid groups on the respective functional silanes, APTES and CSPTMS, provided imprinted cavities with sufficient positive and negative charges to stabilize the AMF template, whose amino and phosphoric acid groups were also dissociated at pH 7.5 to make it amphiprotic, as shown in Fig. 4. The steady electrostatic interaction increased the quenching efficacy and thus the quenching ratio. The effect of the concentration of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP, which was dispersed in phosphate buffer at pH 7.5 and prepared at 0.2–0.8 mg/mL, on the quenching efficacy is shown in Fig. 5B. The 0.5-mg/mL solution possessed the highest quenching ratio and was thus the most highly sensitive to the change in fluorescence quenching caused by AMF. It required time to reach absorption equilibrium between the MIP and template. The dependence of the quenching ratio on the equilibrium time, which was monitored from 10 to 140 s after addition of 60 nM AMF to 0.5 mg/mL CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP in the buffer solution at pH 7.5, is shown in Fig. 5C. The result shows that 2 min was enough to reach equilibrium. The equilibrium time of 2 min is comparable to the times of other silica-based MIPs (Ensafi et al., 2018a; 2018b). Based on the optimized conditions, the relative standard deviation (RSD) in the fluorescence intensity for eight replicate measurements using 60 nM AMF solutions was 3.0%.

Under the optimized sensing conditions, the fluorescence of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP was quenched by different concentrations of AMF ranging from 0.5 nM to 200 nM, and the fluorescence spectra are presented in Fig. 6 A. Fig. 6B shows that the fluorescence quenching was fitted by two linear equations in which F 0/F = 0.0676 C 1 + 0.998 (R2 = 0.9990) was applicable to C 1 in the low-level range of 0.5–10 nM whereas F 0/F = 0.0359 C 2 + 1.035 (R2 = 0.9991) was applicable to C 2 in the high-level range of 20–200 nM. Two linear relationships could be suggested by the inhomogeneity of the imprinted cavities on the CD-MIP surfaces because three different silanes were simultaneously involved in cavity formation (Wu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2017b). Both linearities conformed to the Stern−Volmer equation, F 0/F = K [Q] + 1, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively, [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher, and K is the Stern–Volmer constant. According to 3S/N (RSD = 3.3%, blank samples (n = 10)), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.15 nM, which was below 0.12 μM (Na et al., 2015) and 0.4 nM (Li et al., 2018b). As to the CD-APTES/CSPTMS-NIPs, their non-specific binding with AMF was increased with AMF concentration in the narrow range of 2.0–15 nM and reached to constant at higher concentrations than 15 nM under the same optimized sensing conditions, as shown in Fig. S6.

AMF can be converted to the metabolite 2-(3-aminopropylamino)ethanethiol by ALP. The quenching effect of AMF on CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP was lessened and the fluorescence intensity recovered as the ALP activity for the dephosphorylation of AMF increased. Fig. 6C shows the increase in the fluorescence intensity (F) with increasing concentration of ALP ([ALP]), which was added to the standard AMF solution (300 nM) in phosphate buffer (pH 7.5, 50 mM). As presented in Fig. 6D, the calibration curve showed good linearity (F = 5.1218 [ALP] + 88.423; R 2 = 0.9986) in the range of [ALP] = 2–150 μU/mL. Based on 3S/N (RSD = 3.5%, blank samples (n = 8)), the LOD was calculated to be 0.5 μU/mL, which was below 45 μU/mL (Na et al., 2018) and 20 μU/mL (Luo et al., 2019). Although a LOD of 2 nU/mL was reported previously, there was no description of whether [ALP] referred to the ALP concentrations prepared before (as in this study) or after mixing with AMF and the fluorescent probe (Na et al., 2015).

Human serum was used as the sample matrix to evaluate the recovery of spiked AMF after treatment with trichloroacetic acid and sodium orthovanadate to remove interfering proteins and to inactivate the ALP activity, respectively. Table S1 shows that the recoveries of 1, 10, and 60 nM AMF from the 360-fold-diluted human serum solutions were 95, 104, and 103%, respectively, with RSD values that were lower than 4.2%. The method can determine AMF in the whole blood of patients after receiving a 1-min bolus injection of 600 mg/m2 of amifostine (Bai et al., 2002).

The dephosphorylation activity of the 1200-fold-diluted deproteinized serum at different volumes was evaluated by mixing standard ALP of specified volumes up to 0.1 mL as samples. Table S2 shows that the average ALP activity was 26.71 μU/mL (RSD = 5.41%) in diluted serum and 32.1 U/L in original human serum, for which the concentration of ALP is normally in the range of 20 U/L to 140 U/L depending on the dephosphorylation conditions, such as pH, temperature, and duration (Li et al., 2017).

The synthetic CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP was superior to CD-APTES-MIP and CD-CSPTMS-MIP in terms of quenching ratio and imprinted factor when interacted with the AMF template and compared with the NIP counterparts, respectively. AMF is an amphiprotic aminothiophosphate and is properly imprinted in the amphiprotic cavity of CD-APTES/CSPTMS-MIP. After optimizing the sensing conditions, including the pH, sensor concentration, and equilibration time, the calibration curves of AMF and ALP were determined based on fluorescence quenching and restoration, respectively. The recovery of spiked AMF from human serum solutions was satisfactory, and the ALP activity in human serum was determined to be 32.1 U/L (RSD = 5.41%).

Ching-Bin Ke: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Te-Ling Lu: Visualization, Resources, Software. Jian-Lian Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Support for this work from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under Grant no. MOST 107-2113-M-039-004 and the China Medical University under Grant no. CMU107-S-45 is gratefully acknowledged.
